allocations.rationale: Core growth engine. 26.9% 3yr CAGR with 18.6% alpha — manager identifies undervalued mid-sized stocks with 10-bagger potential. 6-year track record proves skill, not luck. Low tracking error (13%) indicates conviction bets, not closet indexing.

Quality compounders with lower risk. 25.0% 3yr CAGR, but β=0.89 (takes 11% less risk than market). 14.5% alpha over 5 years. Manager favours profitable, growing mid-caps. Tighter tracking error (7.8%) indicates quality focus. Portfolio ballast against excessive volatility.

Where wealth creation explodes. 25.2% 3yr CAGR, 22.2% 5yr CAGR. Manager has found genuine small-cap winners. 10.8% alpha. This is the 10-bagger zone — where Peter Lynch made his wealth. Only 7 years old but 22% 5yr CAGR is exceptional.

Tax-efficient wealth builder with Section 80C deduction. ₹1.5L/year investment saves ₹45k tax (30% slab). 19% 3yr CAGR, 21.8% 5yr CAGR, 18.6% alpha. Equity-taxed gains (12.5% on gains >₹1.25L) are favourable. 3-year lock-in aligns with daughter's education goal. Best of both worlds: tax deduction + growth.

High-conviction defensive quality. Manager picks only ~20-30 stocks (concentrated conviction), β=0.55 (takes 45% LESS risk than market). 16.7% 5yr CAGR, 11.6% alpha. When markets crash 30%, this drops only ~15%. Founder (Parag Parikh) is legendary value investor. Portfolio insurance.

Anchor: stable dividend growers and earnings compounders. 18.2% 3yr CAGR, 17.7% 5yr CAGR, 4.3% alpha. β=0.98 (market-tracking). Holds large, profitable Indian companies (HDFC, Bajaj, TCS class stocks). Lower volatility, proven 18-year history. Ballast when mid/small caps tumble.

Portfolio insurance and inflation hedge. 25.3% 5yr CAGR. Gold acts as shock absorber — when equities fall 20-30%, gold often rises. Keeps you from panic-selling in downturns. Slab-taxed but 0.4% fee is negligible.
Opportunity fund. Keep in liquid savings or ultra-short-term ETFs. Deploy when market drops 15-20%+ (rebalancing opportunities).

review_checkpoints: Quarterly (every 3 months): Check NAV, 1-year returns vs category benchmark (Nifty 50, Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250). Red flag: fund underperforms category for 2 consecutive quarters (investigate manager change or thesis breakdown). Green flag: beating benchmark by 3-5%.
Every 6 months: Review fund factsheet—top 10 holdings, portfolio turnover, sector allocation. Verify portfolio coherence matches manager's stated philosophy. Read fund's recent investor update.

Annually (end of financial year): Full NAV restatement, tax breakdown (gains/losses by fund for tax-loss harvesting), 1y/3y/5y CAGR trend review. Track: Is 5-year CAGR still >20% for mid/small caps? If drops to <18% for 2 consecutive years, consider replacement.

Quarterly ritual (30 minutes): (1) Pull NAV from platform. (2) Calculate quarterly return: (Current NAV − Previous NAV) ÷ Previous NAV. (3) Compare vs Nifty 50/Midcap 150/Smallcap 250. (4) Read latest fund factsheet from fund house. (5) Note: Does manager commentary explain underperformance (market downturn) or manager underperformance (bad stock picks)?

Annual rebalancing (December / end of FY): Rebalance allocations back to target if any fund drifts >20% or <8%. Use 'Switch' feature within AMC (zero tax) or redeem+repurchase across AMCs. Document tax impact (STCG vs LTCG). Lock in long-term gains gradually.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Initial allocation at portfolio inception. Aggressive equity-heavy stance appropriate for 38-year-old with 20-year horizon.
Rebalance after 5 years if allocations drift >5%. Education goal checkpoint at year 10; maintain aggressive posture for remaining 15-year retirement horizon.
Daughter's education funding milestone (₹2+ crore target reached). Slight defensive shift: modest gold increase. 10 years to retirement; maintain equity dominance.
Only 2-3 years to retirement. Gradual de-risking: introduce 5% debt allocation (if retirement corpus target already exceeded in bull case). Lock-in gains in debt to reduce sequence-of-returns risk.

Target retirement year (age 55). Final de-risking: 75% equity / 10% debt / 5% gold. If corpus targets met, shift retirement corpus to fixed income. Continue SIP in equity only if retiring later than age 55.

aps.reasoning: passive_instrument_fraction: All 6 equity funds (Motilal Oswal Large & Midcap, HDFC Mid Cap, Invesco Smallcap, Sundaram ELSS, Parag Parikh Flexi Cap, Nippon Large Cap) are actively managed with explicit alpha mandates. Zero passive/index equity exposure. Score = 0.0. turnover_score: Quarterly monitoring rituals are specified, but actual rebalancing is annual with a drift trigger (>20% or <8%). The buffer fund with tactical deployment on 15-20% market drops adds mild tactical activity. This lands in the middle-low range for passivity — not monthly/quarterly rebalancing, but not purely buy-and-hold either. Score = 0.45. cost_emphasis_score: Cost is barely mentioned — the only reference is a passing note that the Gold ETF's 0.4% expense ratio is 'negligible.' Fund selection is entirely alpha/return/manager-quality driven. Score = 0.08. research_vs_cost_score: Extremely research-intensive plan — discusses manager philosophy (Parag Parikh as 'legendary value investor'), beta/alpha metrics per fund, tracking error as proxy for conviction, references to Peter Lynch and '10-bagger' stock picking. Deeply active research orientation. Score = 0.05. time_horizon_alignment_score: 20-year horizon with an explicit glide path from year 0 to year 17, SIP-based accumulation, and long-term compounding thesis throughout. Quarterly reviews are monitoring, not trading triggers. Score = 0.7. portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds show high alpha (4.3%–18.6%) and elevated tracking errors vs Nifty 50 (7.8%–13%). Beta ranges from 0.55 (Parag Parikh) to 0.98 (Nippon Large Cap), reflecting deliberate active tilts. The plan explicitly celebrates high tracking error as 'conviction bets, not closet indexing.' Gold ETF is passive by nature but only 4% of portfolio. Overall portfolio is genuinely and emphatically active. Score = 0.08.

pqs.reasoning: GOAL ALIGNMENT (0.74): The 87% equity allocation and aggressive mid/small-cap tilt align well with the 20-year horizon and aggressive appetite. ELSS correctly targets the ₹1.5L 80C limit for the 30% slab investor. The de-risking schedule addresses both the 10-year education milestone and retirement at 55. However, no corpus projections are provided to validate feasibility of the ₹8Cr retirement target or the education corpus, which is a material gap for a data-driven investor who explicitly wants evidence-based rationale. The education goal is partially addressed but not quantified. DIVERSIFICATION (0.68): Good spread across large, large-mid, mid, small, flexi-cap, and ELSS categories with seven distinct fund houses. PPFAS provides meaningful international diversification. However, zero debt allocation across the entire 20-year plan is a notable gap — even at year 0 there is no fixed-income component. The 9% 'buffer' in liquid/money market is not a true diversifier. Sector/thematic diversification is not discussed. RISK-RETURN APPROPRIATENESS (0.71): The equity-heavy tilt suits an aggressive 38-year-old with a 20-year runway. The PPFAS inclusion (β=0.55) and buffer provide some drawdown cushion. However, there are no projected return scenarios (base/bear/bull), no quantification of sequence-of-returns risk near retirement, and no downside scenario analysis — all of which this data-driven investor explicitly needs. INTERNAL CONSISTENCY (0.62): Several contradictions undermine the plan. Motilal Oswal's 13% tracking error is described as 'low' when it is actually quite high for a Large & Midcap fund — low tracking error means closet indexing, not conviction. HDFC Midcap and PPFAS are both described as 'ballast/insurance' alongside gold — three instruments competing for the same narrative role. Nippon Large Cap is called 'lower volatility' despite β=0.98. The allocation schedule introduces debt at year 15 but names no specific debt funds, leaving execution undefined. The ELSS 3-year lock-in is cited as aligning with the education goal (10 years away) — a weak logical link. TAX EFFICIENCY (0.72): The plan correctly identifies the 30% slab status and sizes ELSS SIP at ₹18,000/month (₹2.16L/year, though 80C limit is ₹1.5L — the excess provides no 80C benefit, though it still gets equity taxation). Tax treatment of equity gains (12.5% LTCG) is acknowledged. Gold's slab-taxed nature is noted without penalty. However, the 9% buffer allocated to liquid/ultra-short funds is slab-taxed at 30% — the plan misses the opportunity to use arbitrage funds (equity-taxed at 20% STCG / 12.5% LTCG) for the same liquidity purpose, a meaningful inefficiency for a 30% slab investor. No mention of tax-loss harvesting strategy beyond a brief annual review note.
